claims.claim_text: AI Act Article 4 requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures ensuring sufficient AI literacy of staff and other persons acting on their behalf.
claims.notes: This is the core regulatory basis for the product.

claims.claim_text: AI literacy measures should reflect technical knowledge, experience, education, training and the context in which AI systems are used.
claims.notes: The Commission's AI literacy Q&A makes the context-sensitive interpretation explicit.

claims.claim_text: House of Training offers practical AI fundamentals training that can substitute for generic AI literacy workshops.
claims.notes: House of Training publicly lists a 4h face-to-face generative AI fundamentals course in French.

claims.claim_text: Elements of AI is a free online substitute with no attendance requirements or exams.
claims.notes: The course description states that it is completed online with no attendance requirements or exams.

claims.claim_text: EU-wide coverage applies to all companies.
claims.notes: The AI Act applies to providers and deployers of AI systems and related actors in scope, not to every company in the EU by default.
claims.next_action: falsify fully and rewrite or drop

claims.claim_text: Most AI literacy training is English-only.
claims.notes: The reviewed substitutes include a French public offering and a multilingual corporate market example, so the blanket language claim is not proven.
claims.next_action: treat as open question, not as fact

claims.claim_text: EU-wide remote delivery is enough to solve the buyer's AI literacy problem.
claims.notes: The product may still need local policy and country-context adaptation.
claims.next_action: treat as open question, not as fact

claims.claim_text: The multilingual FR/DE/EN angle is a durable geographic advantage.
claims.notes: The advantage is plausible but not externally established.
claims.next_action: treat as open question, not as fact
